g.data-narration: Welcome to Write Buffers in Microcontrollers. This module covers essential concepts for efficient embedded system design.
Write buffers temporarily store data before writing to slow memory or peripherals, allowing the CPU to continue without waiting.
g.text: Problem: CPU (fast) must wait for slow memory/peripheral writes
Solution: Buffer decouples CPU from slow operations, improving throughput
CPU continues immediately after writing to buffer

g.data-narration: A write buffer is a small high-speed memory between CPU and destination, storing pending write operations in a queue.
Write buffers contain buffer memory, address registers, control logic, and status flags for managing write operations.
Write-through writes to buffer and memory simultaneously. Write-back caches data, writes later for better performance.
g.text: Writes to buffer AND memory simultaneously
Caches data, writes to memory later

g.data-narration: FIFO buffers preserve write order: first data in is first data out. Use write pointer, read pointer, and count.
Ring buffers use wraparound addressing where pointers wrap to start. Efficient for UART, ADC, and interrupt handling.
Flush operations transfer buffered data to destination. Triggered by full buffer, explicit flush, or power-down.
UART transmit uses ring buffer to queue characters. CPU adds data, interrupt sends when UART is ready.
g.text: 1. Check buffer full → wait if full
2. Write data at head, advance head

g.data-narration: ADC double buffering allows continuous sampling. ISR fills one buffer while main code processes the other.
ARM Cortex-M includes integrated write buffer, configurable via ACTLR register and MPU for memory region attributes.
g.text: • MPU regions: WT or WB per memory area

g.data-narration: Key exam points: buffer depth affects performance, flush before DMA, use memory barriers, handle buffer-full conditions.
g.text: Buffer ops to prevent race conditions
Ensure data not lost in sleep

g.data-narration: Summary: Write buffers decouple CPU from slow operations using FIFO/ring structures, improving system throughput.
Applications include UART, ADC, DMA. Remember flush operations, memory barriers, and buffer-full checks for exam success.
